titleStmt.title: Letter from Michael O'Connell to the Under-Secretary, Dublin Castle, 22 April 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Michael O'Connell

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: A letter from Michael O'Connell, a medical practitioner, to Sir Matthew Nathan, the Under-Secretary of Ireland. O'Connell writes that his allowance of petrol is insufficient for him to carry out his work. He requests a form to acquire more petrol for his medical practice and for the practice of a colleague who is currently on military service in France.Sir Matthew Nathan was the under-secretary for Ireland from 1914 to 1916. In 1916 Nathan offered his resignation following the outbreak of the Easter Rising. Under the Defence of the Realm Act (DORA), introduced in 1914, restrictions on the use of petrol throughout the British isles were steadily tightened as the war continued.

correspAction.placeName: 3 Coote Terrace, Maryborough, Portlaoise, County Laois, Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
3 Coote Terrace, Maryborough. 22 â 4 â 1916 To Under â Secretary Dublin Castle Sir, I beg to inform you that
that I am a medical
practitioner, and that
in addition of my own
work I am performing
the duties of a colleague
who is with our armies
in France. My
consumption of petrol
in six months is
from 250 to 300 gallons.
At the present time I
am unable to get
sufficient to carry on
my work. I have
returned the form that
you forwarded to medical
practitioners. Your medical servant, Michael O'Connell MD
